macro_segments.title: Introduction, Personal Remarks, and Opening the New Daf Yomi Cycle

micro_segments.title: Welcome to the new Daf Yomi cycle and marking the next Siyum HaShas date
Personal family dedications: Bar Mitzvah, grandfather's Shas, and the value of generational Torah transmission
Reading the Mishna: Me'eimatai korin et Shema be'arvin — the three opinions on the end-time of evening Shema
The debate about the end-time of evening Shema: Rabi Eliezer, Chachamim, and Rabban Gamliel — rooted in the verse beshochbecha uvekumecha
Rabban Gamliel's sons return from a party and the ruling that Shema may be said all night
The Mishna's principle: wherever Chachamim said 'until midnight,' the real mitzvah is until dawn — with examples from sacrificial fats and eating korbanot
The rabbinic enactment of midnight as a fence against transgression — leharchik adam min ha'aveira
Rashi's comment on the burning of fats and the question of whether the midnight limit applies to it; whether the final clause belongs to Rabban Gamliel or to the Mishna

macro_segments.title: Why Berakhot Begins Shas: Structure of the Six Orders and the Meaning of Daily Life

micro_segments.title: Why does Shas begin with Berakhot and with Zeraim? Tractates ordered by size within each seder
Zeraim as the order of daily life in an agricultural society — and Berakhot as prayer and blessings within that framework
The progressive structure of all six sedarim: daily life → special days → family → society → Mikdash
What is implicit in the first Mishna: entering the conversation already in progress, and the dual role of the rabbis as interpreters and legislators
The Mishna introduces rabbinic legislation as fence-making (va'asu mishmeret lemishmarti) alongside biblical obligation
Question from the floor about Shemoneh Esrei — why doesn't the Mishna begin there? The logical organization of Berakhot

macro_segments.title: Gemara's Opening Questions: Why Start with Evening Shema, and the Two Models for Understanding Shema Times

micro_segments.title: Tanna heicha kai: where was the Tanna standing that he asked 'from when'? The Gemara's implicit assumption of prior knowledge
Why does the Mishna discuss evening Shema before morning Shema? — Two answers: the verse beshochbecha uvekumecha, and vayehi erev vayehi voker
The Gemara's resolution of the ABBA chiastic structure: the Tanna opens with night, shifts to morning, then returns to night for the berachot
The two models of Shema time implied by the two verses: night-and-day model vs. going-to-sleep-and-waking-up model

macro_segments.title: When Do Kohanim Eat Terumah? Establishing Tzeit HaKochavim as Nightfall

micro_segments.title: Why doesn't the Mishna simply say 'from when the stars come out' instead of citing the Kohanim eating terumah? — Milta agav orcha: it teaches us in passing
The Tosefta that explicitly states 'siman ladavar tzeit hakochavim' — resolving how we know the Kohanim eat at nightfall
The symbolic significance: citing the Kohanim coming home implies the going-to-sleep model, not the halakhic night-and-day model
Masechet Berakhot as a 'slice of life': Kohanim, poor people, travelers, ערב שבת — the verses of beshivtecha and uvelechtecha
Discussion: Were Kohanim actually eating terumah when the Mishna was written? Terumah after the destruction and transferring kedushah to post-Mikdash life
Discussion: What does 'nichnasim' mean — where are the Kohanim entering from? The mikveh on the outskirts
Question: Is the introduction of Kohanim and sacrificial fats a way of framing Shema/Tefillah as avodah shebalev?

macro_segments.title: Kaparah Lo Me'akva: Deriving That Sunset Plus Stars — Not Sacrifice — Permits Eating Terumah

micro_segments.title: The Gemara's second teaching derived in passing: kaparah lo me'akva — a pending sacrifice does not prevent eating terumah after nightfall
The verse uva hashemesh vetaher and the question: does it mean sunset (bi'at shimsho) or the person purifying himself through sacrifice (taher gavra)?
Rabbah bar Rav Shila's answer: the verse would say veyitaher if human action were required — vetaher means taher yoma, the day is fully cleansed
The folk expression cited: i'arev shimsha ve'idchi yoma — the sun set and the day has been cleansed
In the West (Israel), the question was asked afresh without knowledge of Rabbah bar Shila's answer — resolved instead from the Braita: siman ladavar tzeit hakochavim
Two modes of rabbinic tradition: deriving meaning from verses (hermeneutics) vs. transmitted rabbinic ruling — and the Gemara's preference for the hermeneutic engagement

macro_segments.title: Multiple Descriptions of When Evening Shema Begins: Kohen, Poor Man, and Middle Class — Are They the Same Time?

micro_segments.title: The Baraita: mishe'ani nichnas le'echol pito bemelach — the poor man eating bread with salt as the start time of evening Shema
The Tosefta: mishe'bnei adam nichnasim le'echol pitam be'arvei Shabbatot — Rabbi Meir vs. Chachamim on normal people entering on Erev Shabbat

macro_segments.title: Kaparah Lo Me'akva: Deriving That Sunset Plus Stars — Not Sacrifice — Permits Eating Terumah (continued)

micro_segments.title: The verse from Nechemiah about spear-holders from alot hashachar until tzeit hakochavim — zecher ladavar, not a proof from Torah
Why a second verse was needed: to exclude the possibility that the workers went extra early and late, beyond the natural day

macro_segments.title: Multiple Descriptions of When Evening Shema Begins: Kohen, Poor Man, and Middle Class — Are They the Same Time? (continued)

micro_segments.title: Are the poor man's time and the Kohen's time the same? The Gemara tries to reconcile but is forced to conclude they are different
The full Braita listing all opinions: Rabbi Eliezer (kidush hayom), Rabbi Yehoshua (kohanim zakka'in), Rabbi Meir (kohanim tovlin), Rabbi Chanina (ani), Rabbi Achai (rov bnei adam lehaseiv)
Social observation: three tiers — Kohanim, poor, and middle class — as models for the start of evening Shema, and the question of whose daily life sets the standard
The Gemara's logical resolution: since Rabbi Chanina and Rabbi Yehoshua appear to argue, the poor man's time and the Kohen's time must be distinct
Rabbi Yehuda challenges Rabbi Meir: the Kohanim immerse while it is still day — how can that be the start of the night Shema?
Rabbi Meir's response: I follow Rabbi Yosi's bein hashmashot — a split second before tzeit, so immersion is after sunset but still halachically day
Which time is later — the poor man's or the Kohen's? The Gemara concludes the poor man eats even later than tzeit hakochavim because he works longer
Rabbi Yosi: bein hashmashot keheref ayin — an imperceptible instant; and the two conflicting traditions about Rabbi Meir and Rabbi Eliezer (trei tannai aliba)

topical_tags.term: Ad Chatzot / Leharchik Adam Min Ha'aveira
